text: “My mirrorwright has reminded me that we have not yet brought up the attacks upon Tregard’s ships and villages along Elleron’s waters,” Rickard said. “Should we dispense with the trivialities and discuss recompense?” “Ah, yes, these alleged attacks. Why would there be recompense? These raids are obviously the result of pirates. The Isles have always had an issue with brigands. It is part of the risk of doing business on these waters. You know this.” “Because these attacks aren’t being done by pirates, as you well know.” Ergant frowned in consternation. “I don’t know what you’re talking about.” Rickard slammed a fist down on the table. “I have reports from the few ships that managed to escape and the survivors in some of the villages. The ‘pirates’ were too well-organized, too well-equipped. The men claimed they had military experience, that they moved like soldiers.” “Were they wearing Elleron uniforms?” “No.” Aurel shot Rickard a bewildered look, hand clutching at the cloth in his pocket. “Then I fail to see what evidence you have that I had anything to do with it. Soldiers have been known to abandon their posts. It’s not hard to believe some of them became pirates. One of them may have even trained his own band of brigands.” “Don’t lie to me, Ergant. I know you’re the one behind these attacks. I’ve lost seven ships to these ‘pirates.’ Two more barely escaped with their lives and the ship intact. And what of the villages? Argo, Teppit, Grean, Ival, a half dozen more…all of them razed to the ground, the villagers slaughtered. And for what? Nothing was taken but a few meager loads of fish. The villagers had nothing of value. The only thing they had in common was location—along Elleron-Tregard waters!” Ergant was shaking his head. “Elleron had nothing to do with this. I had nothing to do with this.” “And what of Istabol?” Aurel demanded, stepping forward, voice rising. The casualness of Ergant’s lie and ease of his dismissal of the matter infuriated him, overrode ingrained protocol. “What of Istabol, you bastard?” “Istabol?” Ergant appeared honestly confused, glancing down at the now crumpled and worn list of Rickard’s demands, items notated and marked off. “There is no Istabol on this list.” “Istabol! A small island on the edge of our mutual border, tucked into a corner, useless for anything except fishing. Not even on a trade route. And yet you used it as a target. You sent your soldiers there and had them butcher the villagers. They killed everyone, including my wife, her family, the children…everyone.” Aurel had advanced down the length of the table, had halted in the middle by force of habit. He stood there, trembling in rage. “I don’t know anyth—” “Don’t deny it!” Aurel roared, snatching the cloth from his pocket and throwing it onto the table. It landed with the embroidered gold oak leaf face up, on a swatch of torn blood-red fabric. “You can’t deny it! I found this clutched in my wife’s hand where her corpse had been thrown to the sand outside her family’s hut. These weren’t pirates. Your soldiers were there!” Ergant had stilled, his gaze fixed on Aurel. It dropped to the cloth and his brow creased. He began to move around the table, Utak shifting to stop him. He thrust her aside and stalked down its length to stand before Aurel, reaching for the cloth. Utak followed at his heels, her eyes locked on Aurel. Ergant held the swatch in one hand, ran his hand over the embroidery, stained with Elean’s blood. Aurel shook, hands clenched. Ergant met his gaze. “This is part of one of our uniforms, but I swear that I have never heard of Istabol before today.” Something inside Aurel broke. He reached for Ergant with a low growl. He didn’t know what he intended—to gouge out his eyes with his bare fingers, clamp his mouth down on his throat and bite and tear until he tasted royal blood—but Utak stopped him, her hands seizing his wrists as she interposed herself between them. She let him struggle against her and said calmly, “No bloodshed in this room.” He gave a ragged cry and thrust back from her, panting, eyes still locked on Ergant. “He’s lying. We have the proof.” “You have a bloodied cloth,” Utak said. “That is not evidence. That is not truth.” A scream began to well up inside Aurel—built of frustration and pain—and then he caught sight of the mirror beside them. He met Utak’s eyes. “There is one way to find the truth.” Confusion caused her to pause and in that void of uncertainty Aurel muttered an incantation and reached for Ergant. He flung himself to the side, dragging Ergant with him, slipping into the mirror. Comprehension dawned on Utak’s face a moment before they vanished. The world flashed a refracted gray and the taste of blood and ash and limestone coated Aurel’s throat. He staggered back, caught Ergant’s weight against him and held it tight as the sensation of glass slicing across flesh settled over him—over them both, he knew. Ergant bellowed and began to thrash, but Aurel held on fiercely. “Don’t!” he shouted. “It’s too late! You’re already here.” Ergant fought a moment more, then slowed, breath heaving. “Where have you taken me? This isn’t possible. The chamber was sealed.” Aurel released him. “We haven’t left the chamber. We’re inside the glass of the mirror, the space between the chamber and the silvering at its back. But at the same time we’re outside it, in a space between, a world between. You cannot hide here. You cannot lie . The glass allows only the truth.” They stood amidst thousands of floating shards, glittering with reflected silver light, iridescent rainbows forming and vanishing as the shards shifted and rotated with a frisson of sound like ice slowly cracking. It burrowed into Aurel’s skull, juddered in his teeth, made them ache. In the flat surface of one of
